name: UNIX Terminal Essentials
description: The commands a programmer actually types every day: navigating, viewing, searching, pipes and redirection, processes, permissions, networking, and archives, plus the classic traps (rm has no undo, > overwrites, kill -9, uniq needs sort).

fields.front: Which command prints the absolute path of the directory you are currently in?
fields.extra: Short for *print working directory*.

fields.front: Which command moves your shell to a different directory?
fields.extra: `cd /path` for absolute paths, `cd sub` for relative. It is a shell builtin, not a program.

fields.front: Where does `cd` with no arguments take you?
fields.back: Your home directory.
fields.extra: Same as `cd ~`. The location is stored in `$HOME`.

fields.front: What does `cd -` do?
fields.back: Jumps back to the directory you were in before the last `cd`.
fields.extra: It also prints where it landed. Handy for bouncing between two places.

fields.front: In a path, what does `..` refer to?
fields.back: The parent directory, one level up.
fields.extra: `.` is the current directory; `cd ../..` climbs two levels.

fields.front: Which command lists the files in a directory?
fields.extra: With no argument it lists the current directory.

fields.front: What does `ls -l` show that plain `ls` does not?
fields.back: The long format: one file per line with permissions, owner, size, and modification time.
fields.extra: The first column (`-rwxr-xr--`) is the permission string; later cards decode it.

fields.front: Which files does `ls -a` reveal that plain `ls` hides?
fields.back: Hidden files: names starting with a dot, like `.gitignore`.
fields.extra: `-A` is the same but drops the `.` and `..` entries.

fields.front: In `ls -lh`, what does the `h` flag change?
fields.back: Sizes print human-readable (`4.0K`, `2.3M`) instead of raw byte counts.
fields.extra: The same `-h` works on `df` and `du`.

fields.front: Which command opens the reference manual for another command?
fields.extra: Inside: space pages down, `/pattern` searches, `q` quits. `man ls` beats guessing flags.

fields.front: What does `which python` print?
fields.back: The full path of the executable that would run when you type `python`.
fields.extra: Great for debugging 'wrong version runs' problems: it searches `$PATH` in order.

fields.front: Which command creates a new directory?
fields.extra: *make directory*.

fields.front: What does `mkdir -p a/b/c` do that plain `mkdir a/b/c` cannot?
fields.back: It creates the missing parent directories `a` and `a/b` along the way.
fields.extra: `-p` also silences the 'File exists' error, so it is safe to rerun in scripts.

fields.front: What does `touch notes.txt` do when the file does not exist?
fields.back: Creates it, empty.
fields.extra: On an existing file it updates the modification timestamp, which is its original purpose.

fields.front: Which command copies a file?
fields.extra: `cp source destination`: source comes first.

fields.front: Which flag does `cp` need before it will copy a directory?
fields.back: `-r` (recursive): `cp -r src/ dest/`.
fields.extra: Without it, `cp` refuses directories outright.

fields.front: How do you rename `draft.txt` to `final.txt`?
fields.back: `mv draft.txt final.txt`
fields.extra: There is no separate rename command: `mv` does both moving and renaming, and needs no `-r` for directories.

fields.front: Which command deletes a file?
fields.extra: *remove*.

fields.front: You just deleted the wrong file with `rm`. How do you undo it?
fields.back: You cannot: `rm` bypasses any trash, so the file is simply gone.
fields.extra: This is why `rm -rf` has its reputation. `-i` prompts before each delete when you want a safety net.

fields.front: Which flag makes `rm` delete a directory and everything inside it?
fields.back: `-r`, as in `rm -r build/`.
fields.extra: Adding `-f` (force) skips all prompts and missing-file errors: `rm -rf` is powerful and unforgiving.

fields.front: What does `ln -s /opt/app/config.yml config.yml` create?
fields.back: A symbolic link named `config.yml` that points to `/opt/app/config.yml`.
fields.extra: Argument order: real target first, link name second (same direction as `cp`).

fields.front: Which command prints a file's entire contents straight to the terminal?
fields.extra: Short for *concatenate*: `cat a b > combined` joins files.

fields.front: Why open a long file with `less` instead of `cat`?
fields.back: `less` pages the file, letting you scroll and search instead of dumping everything at once.
fields.extra: Keys: space pages down, `/pattern` searches, `q` quits. `man` uses it under the hood.

fields.front: What does `head app.log` print by default?
fields.back: The first 10 lines.
fields.extra: `head -n 50` for the first 50.

fields.front: What does `tail app.log` print by default?
fields.back: The last 10 lines.
fields.extra: Usually what you want from a log.

fields.front: How do you watch a log file grow in real time?
fields.back: `tail -f app.log`: it keeps printing new lines as they are appended.
fields.extra: Ctrl-C stops it. `-F` additionally survives log rotation.

fields.front: What does `wc -l file` count?
fields.back: Lines.
fields.extra: `-w` counts words, `-c` bytes. `grep pattern file | wc -l` counts matches.

fields.front: What does `diff old.txt new.txt` show?
fields.back: The lines that differ between the two files.
fields.extra: `diff -u` gives the +/- unified format you know from git.

fields.front: Which command prints its arguments to standard output?
fields.extra: `echo $PATH` is the quickest way to inspect a variable.

fields.front: In `ps aux | grep node`, what does the `|` do?
fields.back: Feeds the left command's output in as the right command's input.
fields.extra: Pipes are how small tools compose into one-liners; almost every card here chains with them.

fields.front: `ls > files.txt` runs while `files.txt` already exists. What happens to the old contents?
fields.back: They are erased: `>` truncates the file before writing.
fields.extra: A classic way to lose data. Use `>>` when you mean to keep what is there.

fields.front: Which redirection operator adds to the end of a file instead of overwriting it?
fields.back: `>>`, as in `echo done >> log.txt`.
fields.extra: It creates the file if missing, just like `>`.

fields.front: Which output stream does `2>` redirect?
fields.back: Standard error (stderr), stream number 2.
fields.extra: `command > out.txt 2>&1` sends both stdout and stderr into the file.

fields.front: What happens to output redirected to `/dev/null`?
fields.back: It is discarded.
fields.extra: `command 2> /dev/null` silences error spam; the file is a black hole by design.

fields.front: What does `make | tee build.log` do that `make > build.log` does not?
fields.back: It still shows the output on screen while also writing it to the file.
fields.extra: Named after a T-shaped pipe fitting: one stream in, two out.

fields.front: Who expands the `*` in `rm *.log`: the shell or `rm`?
fields.back: The shell; `rm` just receives the matching filenames as ordinary arguments.
fields.extra: That is why patterns meant for `find` or `grep` need quotes: to stop the shell expanding them first.

fields.front: In `make && make test`, when does `make test` run?
fields.back: Only if `make` succeeded (exit status 0).
fields.extra: Read `&&` as 'and then, if that worked'.

fields.front: In `grep -q TODO file || echo clean`, when does the `echo` run?
fields.back: Only if the grep failed (returned a non-zero exit status).
fields.extra: `&&` = and-then; `||` = or-else. `a ; b` runs `b` regardless.

fields.front: Which exit status means a UNIX command succeeded?
fields.back: 0; any non-zero status signals failure.
fields.extra: Backwards from booleans: there is one way to succeed and many numbered ways to fail.

fields.front: Which shell variable holds the exit status of the last command?
fields.extra: Check it immediately (`echo $?`): running anything else overwrites it.

fields.front: In `find . -name '*.tmp' | xargs rm`, what does `xargs` do?
fields.back: Turns the incoming lines into command-line arguments for `rm`.
fields.extra: For filenames containing spaces, use `find ... -print0 | xargs -0`.

fields.front: Which classic UNIX command searches file contents for lines matching a pattern?
fields.extra: `grep TODO src/main.py`. Modern faster take: `rg` (ripgrep).

fields.front: Which `grep` flag makes the match case-insensitive?
fields.extra: `grep -i error app.log` catches Error, ERROR, error.

fields.front: How do you search for a string in every file under a directory tree?
fields.back: `grep -r pattern dir/`
fields.extra: `-r` recurses. Combine with `-n` for line numbers: `grep -rn`.

fields.front: What does `grep -v DEBUG app.log` print?
fields.back: Only the lines that do NOT contain DEBUG.
fields.extra: `-v` inverts the match, turning grep into a filter-out.

fields.front: Which `grep` flag prefixes each match with its line number?
fields.extra: Output like `main.py:42: ...` is clickable in most editors.

fields.front: How do you list every `.log` file anywhere under the current directory?
fields.back: `find . -name '*.log'`
fields.extra: Quote the pattern so the shell does not expand it before `find` sees it.

fields.front: In `find . -type f`, what does `-type f` restrict the results to?
fields.back: Regular files only.
fields.extra: `-type d` restricts to directories.

fields.front: What does `sort names.txt` print?
fields.back: The file's lines, in sorted order.
fields.extra: `-n` sorts numerically, `-r` reverses, `-u` drops duplicates.

fields.front: Why is `uniq` almost always fed sorted input?
fields.back: Because `uniq` only collapses duplicate lines that are adjacent.
fields.extra: Unsorted input leaves scattered duplicates untouched.

fields.front: Which classic pipeline counts how many times each distinct line appears in a file?
fields.back: `sort file | uniq -c`
fields.extra: Append `| sort -rn` to rank by count: an instant leaderboard.

fields.front: What does `cut -d, -f2 data.csv` extract from each line?
fields.back: The second comma-separated field.
fields.extra: `-d` sets the delimiter, `-f` picks the field numbers.

fields.front: What does `awk '{print $2}'` print for each input line?
fields.back: Its second whitespace-separated field.
fields.extra: awk splits on any run of whitespace, which makes it more forgiving than `cut`. `$0` is the whole line.

fields.front: What does `sed 's/http/https/' urls.txt` do?
fields.back: Prints each line with the first occurrence of `http` replaced by `https`.
fields.extra: Add `g` (`s/.../.../g`) to replace every occurrence on a line; `-i` edits the file in place.

fields.front: Which command lists the commands you have previously run in this shell?
fields.extra: `!123` reruns entry 123.

fields.front: Which keystroke incrementally searches your shell history as you type?
fields.back: Ctrl-R
fields.extra: Type a fragment of the old command; press Ctrl-R again to step to older matches.

fields.front: What does `ps aux` list?
fields.back: Every process running on the system, with its owner, PID, and CPU/memory usage.
fields.extra: `ps aux | grep node` narrows it to one program.

fields.front: Which signal does Ctrl-C send to the foreground process?
fields.back: SIGINT, a request to interrupt and quit.
fields.extra: Programs may catch it to clean up before exiting.

fields.front: What does Ctrl-Z do to the foreground process?
fields.back: Suspends (pauses) it and returns you to the prompt.
fields.extra: Resume with `fg` (foreground) or `bg` (keep it running in the background); `jobs` lists what is suspended.

fields.front: What does the trailing `&` do in `npm run dev &`?
fields.back: Runs the command in the background so you get your prompt back immediately.
fields.extra: Its output still lands in your terminal unless redirected; `jobs` lists it, `fg` reclaims it.

fields.front: Which command shows a live, continuously updating view of processes and their CPU/memory use?
fields.back: `top` (or its friendlier cousin `htop`).
fields.extra: `q` quits. Sort and kill from inside htop with function keys.

fields.front: What does `kill 1234` actually do to process 1234?
fields.back: Sends it a signal, SIGTERM by default, which politely asks it to exit.
fields.extra: Despite the name, `kill` is a signal sender; the process may catch SIGTERM and clean up first.

fields.front: When plain `kill` fails, what makes `kill -9` different?
fields.back: It sends SIGKILL, which cannot be caught or ignored: the kernel ends the process immediately.
fields.extra: Last resort only, since the process gets no chance to clean up.

fields.front: What does `pkill node` do?
fields.back: Sends SIGTERM to every process whose name matches `node`.
fields.extra: Preview the targets first with `pgrep node`, which just prints the matching PIDs.

fields.front: Your dev server says port 3000 is already in use. Which command identifies the process holding it?
fields.extra: Then `kill` the PID it prints. `lsof` = list open files, and sockets count as files.

fields.front: Running `./deploy.sh` fails with 'Permission denied'. What is the usual fix?
fields.back: `chmod +x deploy.sh`, which marks the script executable.
fields.extra: A fresh file is never executable by default, even a script.

fields.front: In `chmod 754 file`, which users do the three digits apply to, in order?
fields.back: Owner, group, others.
fields.extra: Same order as the `rwx` triplets in `ls -l` output.

fields.text: In numeric permissions, read = {{c1::4}}, write = {{c2::2}}, execute = {{c3::1}}.
fields.extra: They add up: 7 = rwx, 6 = rw-, 5 = r-x. So 755 = rwxr-xr-x.

fields.front: In the listing `-rwxr-x---`, what can 'others' do with the file?
fields.back: Nothing: the final triplet `---` grants no permissions at all.
fields.extra: Read the string as three triplets after the type character: owner `rwx`, group `r-x`, others `---`.

fields.front: What does prefixing a command with `sudo` change?
fields.back: The command runs as the superuser (root) instead of as you.
fields.extra: Needed for system directories and package installs. Use sparingly: root mistakes are unlimited mistakes.

fields.front: Which command changes which user owns a file?
fields.extra: `chown user:group file`; usually needs `sudo`. *change owner*.

fields.front: Which command prints the username you are currently logged in as?
fields.extra: Useful in scripts and for checking whether you are root.

fields.front: Which command prints all environment variables of your current shell?
fields.back: `env` (or `printenv`).
fields.extra: Pipe through grep to find one: `env | grep AWS`.

fields.front: What does `export API_KEY=abc` do that plain `API_KEY=abc` does not?
fields.back: It passes the variable on to child processes, meaning the commands you run afterwards.
fields.extra: Without `export`, only the shell itself sees the variable; the programs it launches do not.

fields.front: What does the shell use the `$PATH` variable for?
fields.back: It is the ordered list of directories searched to find the program behind a command name.
fields.extra: 'command not found' means no `$PATH` directory contains it; the first match wins.

fields.front: Which command reports how full each disk (filesystem) is?
fields.back: `df -h`
fields.extra: *disk free*; `-h` for readable sizes. Whole-disk view, unlike `du`.

fields.front: How do you find out how much disk space `node_modules/` takes up?
fields.back: `du -sh node_modules`
fields.extra: `-s` gives one total, `-h` readable units. `du -sh * | sort -h` ranks a directory's contents.

fields.front: Which command fetches a URL from the terminal, the standard tool for poking at APIs?
fields.back: `curl`, e.g. `curl https://api.example.com/health`.
fields.extra: `-L` follows redirects, `-i` shows response headers. `wget` is the download-focused alternative.

fields.front: Where does `curl https://x.io/file.tgz` write the download by default?
fields.back: To stdout, meaning your terminal.
fields.extra: `-O` saves under the remote filename; `-o name` picks your own.

fields.front: Which command logs you into a shell on a remote machine?
fields.extra: `ssh user@host`. *secure shell*; key-based auth beats passwords.

fields.front: How do you copy `app.log` from the server `web1` to your current directory?
fields.back: `scp user@web1:app.log .`
fields.extra: `scp` works like `cp` where either side may be `user@host:path`. `rsync` is the resumable, incremental upgrade.

fields.front: What does `ping example.com` measure?
fields.back: Whether the host answers, and the round-trip time of each packet.
fields.extra: Ctrl-C stops it and prints summary stats. First reflex when 'the network is down'.

fields.front: Which command unpacks `release.tar.gz` into the current directory?
fields.back: `tar -xzf release.tar.gz`
fields.extra: Mnemonic: *eXtract Zee Files*. Modern tar auto-detects compression, so `tar xf` alone also works.

fields.front: Which command bundles the directory `data/` into `backup.tar.gz`?
fields.back: `tar -czf backup.tar.gz data/`
fields.extra: *Create Zee Files*. The archive name must come right after `-f`.

fields.front: Which command extracts a `.zip` archive?
fields.extra: `unzip -l archive.zip` lists the contents without extracting.

fields.front: What does `alias gs='git status'` set up?
fields.back: Typing `gs` now runs `git status` in this shell.
fields.extra: `alias` alone lists everything currently defined.

fields.front: Where must an alias live to survive into every new terminal session?
fields.back: In your shell's startup file: `~/.zshrc` for zsh, `~/.bashrc` for bash.
fields.extra: `source ~/.zshrc` applies fresh edits to the shell you are already in.

fields.front: A command just failed because it needed root. What does typing `sudo !!` do?
fields.back: Reruns the previous command with sudo, because `!!` expands to your last command.
fields.extra: History expansion works in both bash and zsh.

fields.front: Which keystroke clears the terminal screen?
fields.back: Ctrl-L
fields.extra: Equivalent to running `clear`, but works mid-typing without losing your input line.

fields.front: What does Ctrl-D at an empty prompt do?
fields.back: Sends end-of-file, which closes the shell (same as typing `exit`).
fields.extra: It is also how you finish typing stdin input to commands like `cat > file`.

fields.front: What does prefixing a command with `time` report when it finishes?
fields.back: How long it took: real (wall clock), user, and sys CPU time.
fields.extra: The quickest benchmark there is: `time make`.

fields.front: You have landed in vim by accident. How do you quit without saving?
fields.back: Press Esc, then type `:q!` and Enter.
fields.extra: `:wq` saves and quits. The eternal Stack Overflow question.

fields.front: On macOS, which command opens a file or URL with its default application?
fields.back: `open`, e.g. `open .` opens the current directory in Finder.
fields.extra: The Linux equivalent is `xdg-open`.
